title: scenario_fire_occupants_refusing_evacuation
description: Occupants refusing to leave a burning structure

timeline.title: Dispatch
timeline.description: Residential fire. Occupants called 911 but are now reported to be refusing to leave.

timeline.title: En Route
timeline.description: Smoke visible from the address. Dispatch confirms occupants are inside and declining to exit.

timeline.title: On Scene
timeline.description: Kitchen fire with smoke filling the home. An adult man and his elderly mother are refusing to leave — man says he can handle it.

timeline.title: Escalating Smoke
timeline.description: Smoke conditions worsening. Man still refusing. Mother appears confused and unable to make independent decision.

timeline.title: Forced Evacuation
timeline.description: Firefighters physically assist both occupants out of the structure.

timeline.title: Fire Controlled
timeline.description: Kitchen fire extinguished. Both occupants assessed by EMS outside.

node-1.title: Initial Occupant Contact
node-1.situation: You arrive to find a kitchen fire with two occupants inside. The adult man says he does not want to leave and believes he can put the fire out himself. His elderly mother appears disoriented. Smoke is filling the home and conditions are deteriorating.
node-1.question: How do you handle the refusing occupant?

options.label: A
options.text: Firmly explain the life-safety risk, advise evacuation as a legal directive, and if they refuse to move, physically assist the elderly mother out first — she lacks capacity to make a safe decision — then focus on the man.

options.label: B
options.text: Respect their decision and allow them to stay while your crew fights the fire around them.

options.label: C
options.text: Leave and let the occupants handle it themselves since they called off the emergency.

node-2.title: Handling the Refusing Adult
node-2.situation: The elderly mother has been assisted outside. The adult man is still refusing to leave and is physically blocking crew entry to the kitchen. Smoke is now at head height in the living room. The man is coughing but still insisting he will stay.
node-2.question: What is your action?

options.label: A
options.text: Physically remove the man from the structure using minimum necessary force — firefighters have authority to remove persons from an IDLH environment. Document the refusal and actions taken.

options.label: B
options.text: Let him stay since he is an adult and can make his own decisions — focus on fire attack only.

options.label: C
options.text: Handcuff the man and wait for police before moving him.

node-3.title: Fire Attack and Documentation
node-3.situation: Both occupants are now outside. The kitchen fire is accessible. You need to extinguish the fire and document the forced evacuation incident properly for your incident report.
node-3.question: How do you proceed?

options.label: A
options.text: Advance the attack line to the kitchen and knock the fire while a crew member ensures the occupants remain with EMS outside. Document the refusal, conditions observed, and actions taken in your incident report.

options.label: B
options.text: Allow the man to re-enter to observe the fire attack once the kitchen is reached.

options.label: C
options.text: Skip documentation since the fire was minor and no injuries occurred.

node-4.title: Scenario Complete
node-4.situation: The kitchen fire was extinguished. Both occupants were evaluated by EMS — the mother showed elevated CO levels. The adult man acknowledged the danger after being assessed by paramedics. Incident documentation was completed detailing the forced evacuation and IDLH conditions justifying the action. Well done — you prioritized life safety, used appropriate authority, and documented your actions correctly.
node-4.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
